figures.caption: FIG. 1. a, Two-particle implementation of the encoded exchange phase. The entangled input contains direct and exchange components, which are mapped by U(x) and read out by label-unresolved correlation detection, giving the two-path response in equation (2). b, Exchange responses selected by the encoded phase ϕ. The cases ϕ = 0, 0 < ϕ < π, and ϕ = π correspond to bosonic, anyonic, and fermionic responses, respectively. c, N -particle fractional quantum kernel workflow. PCA-preprocessed data are encoded into programmable linear-optical unitaries U(x). Acting on the permutation-entangled input |Ψϕ⟩, these unitaries generate fractional-statistics feature states. Their pairwise overlaps define the kernel Gram matrix Kϕ used by a classical SVM with a precomputed quantum kernel.

FIG. 2. a, Effective dimension Deff of the fractional-statistics feature map as a function of the exchange phase ϕ for different particle numbers N . The bosonic and fermionic limits correspond to ϕ = 0 and ϕ = π, respectively, whereas intermediate exchange phases access additional symmetry sectors and enlarge the effective feature space. b, Label-independent geometric difference gCϕ between the fractional-statistics kernel Kϕ and the distinguishable-photon baseline KC . This quantity characterizes the maximum geometry-level separation from the classical baseline and provides an upper bound on the model-complexity reduction that Kϕ can support. c, Realized label-dependent model-complexity ratio Rϕ = sC(y)/sϕ(y) for MNIST and Fashion-MNIST. Values Rϕ > 1 indicate that the fractional-statistics kernel reduces the model complexity relative to the distinguishable-photon baseline for the given labels. In b and c, solid curves denote the mean over ten independent runs, and shaded regions indicate one standard deviation.

FIG. 3. a, Test accuracy of kernel SVM classifiers on MNIST as a function of the exchange phase ϕ. Solid, dashed, and dotted curves correspond to N = 2, 3 and 4 particles, respectively. b, Test accuracy on Fashion-MNIST under the same kernel-SVM protocol and particle-number settings. c, Kernel-target alignment between the fractional-statistics kernel Kϕ and the multiclass target matrix, shown for the two-particle kernel. d, Mean inter-class distance dinter in the kernel-induced feature space for the same setting. Blue and red curves denote MNIST and Fashion-MNIST, respectively. Shaded regions indicate one standard deviation over independent data splits.
